coredata.dc:title: One-step approach to fabrication of PDA-PVA coatings with outstanding antifogging performance assisted by light illumination
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Xiuxian
He, Junhui
coredata.dc:description: Using clean and renewable solar energy, photothermal technology has garnered significant attention from researchers. Photothermal materials possess the capability to efficiently convert solar energy into thermal energy. In this work, polydopamine (PDA)- poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) coatings with outstanding antifogging performance assisted by light illumination were fabricated by a one-step scrape coating method at room temperature. PDA with excellent photothermal properties and strong adhesion to substrate was applied in combination with PVA as matrix. Carbon dots (CDs), which had previously been found as nano-crosslinkers to form abundant hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups of PVA and substrate, was used to enhance the mechanical robustness and the adhesion to substrate of coatings, producing a synergistic effect with the PDA. The coatings had an average transmittance exceeding 80 % in the visible region. Under xenon lamp with light intensities of 60, 80 and 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, the temperature of the coating surface rose to 40.2, 46.1 and 53.1℃, respectively. Under xenon lamp with a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, the antifogging duration when exposed to hot steam 5 cm above a 60 °C water bath was three times more than that without xenon lamp illumination. The coating remained unaffected even after immersion in water and various solvents for 2 h, without showing any signs of folding or damage. The coating exhibited a pencil hardness of up to 8 H and adhesion-to-substrate of up to 5 A, and was able to withstand the Taber wear test for 4000 cycles. The preparation method is simple and efficient, and may be applicable to a wide range of optical, optoelectronic, medical scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polydopamine
Poly(vinyl alcohol)
Antifogging performance
Photothermal properties
One-step method
Room temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and preparation of PDA-PVA coatings
Stability and robustness to water and organic solvents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process of photothermal hygroscopic composite coatings.
SEM top-view images of (a) M-2, (b) M-3 and (c) M-4 (insets: cross-sectional view images). (c) C, (d) O, (e) N element mapping images of M-3. AFM image of (g) M-2, (h) M-3 and (i) M-4.

(a) Transmission spectra of blank glass and different coatings in UV–VIS–NIR region. (b) Absorption spectra of blank glass and different coatings in UV–VIS region. (c) Schematic diagram of coating surface temperature and antifogging measurements setup under xenon lamp illumination. Temperature changes and infrared thermal images of blank glass and different coatings under light intensities of (d) 60 mW cm−2, (e) 80 mW cm−2, and (f) 100 mW cm−2. Correlation between equilibrium temperature (T) and light intensity (L) of (g) M-2, (h) M-3 and (i) M-4 in the range of 50 −100 mW cm−2.

Digital images exhibiting antifogging property under the irradiation of xenon lamp (100 mW cm−2) of blank glass and M-3 5 cm above a 60°C water bath for (a) 10 min, (b) 20 min, (c) 30 min, (d) 40 min, (e) 50 min, (f) 60 min, (d) 70 min, (e) 80 min, (f) 90 min.

Digital images exhibiting antifogging property of coated (left) and uncoated (right) eyeglasses (a) placed at −20°C for 24 h in a refrigerator and then exposed to ambient conditions (25°C, 20 −40 %RH) for 5 s and (b) placed outdoors (Beijing, winter, 2℃) and exposed to hot steam 5 cm above 80°C hot water for 60 s.

(a) Temperature change of M-3 surface with time in twelve light on-off cycles. (b) Maximum (red) and minimum (blue) temperatures of M-3 during the reversible photothermal test. Insets are the corresponding infrared thermal images. (c) Digital photos, (d) temperature evolution and (e-f) temperature difference of the coated and uncoated models under xenon lamp illumination. (g) Side view and (h) top view infrared thermal images of coated and uncoated models under xenon lamp illumination.
Digital photos of M-3 after soaking in various organic solvents for 2 h.

Schematic diagram of (a) the pencil hardness test, (b) the adhesion test, (c) the Taber wear test. Optical photographs after (d) the pencil hardness test, (e) the adhesion test, (f) the Taber wear test of M-3. (g) Transmittances and (h) temperature changes under xenon lamp (100 mW cm−2) of M-3 after undergoing different cycles of sponge scrub. (i) Antifogging images of (left) blank glass and (right) M-3 after undergoing 4000 cycles of sponge scrub. The samples were placed under xenon lamp (100 mW cm−2) and exposed to hot steam 5 cm above a 60 °C water bath for 30 min.

head.ce:title: One-step approach to fabrication of PDA-PVA coatings with outstanding antifogging performance assisted by light illumination

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization
Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using clean and renewable solar energy, photothermal technology has garnered significant attention from researchers. Photothermal materials possess the capability to efficiently convert solar energy into thermal energy. In this work, polydopamine (PDA)- poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) coatings with outstanding antifogging performance assisted by light illumination were fabricated by a one-step scrape coating method at room temperature. PDA with excellent photothermal properties and strong adhesion to substrate was applied in combination with PVA as matrix. Carbon dots (CDs), which had previously been found as nano-crosslinkers to form abundant hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups of PVA and substrate, was used to enhance the mechanical robustness and the adhesion to substrate of coatings, producing a synergistic effect with the PDA. The coatings had an average transmittance exceeding 80 % in the visible region. Under xenon lamp with light intensities of 60, 80 and 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, the temperature of the coating surface rose to 40.2, 46.1 and 53.1℃, respectively. Under xenon lamp with a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, the antifogging duration when exposed to hot steam 5 cm above a 60 °C water bath was three times more than that without xenon lamp illumination. The coating remained unaffected even after immersion in water and various solvents for 2 h, without showing any signs of folding or damage. The coating exhibited a pencil hardness of up to 8 H and adhesion-to-substrate of up to 5 A, and was able to withstand the Taber wear test for 4000 cycles. The preparation method is simple and efficient, and may be applicable to a wide range of optical, optoelectronic, medical scenarios.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fog formation and accumulation will inevitably exert a significant impact on people's daily life [1,2]. Surface fogging is typically ascribed to reduction in ambient temperature or increase in relative humidity, which results in the nucleation of a substantial number of water droplets on the surface [3,4]. The presence of water droplets enhances light scattering, leading to decrease in transparency [5,6]. The current strategies for preventing fogging can be categorized into active and passive approaches. Active antifogging methods involve adjusting environmental parameters, such as temperature and relative humidity, achieving the desired antifogging effect [7,8]. Various transparent heaters are used to raise surface temperature and prevent surface fogging. However, the use of electric heaters requires an external power input, which is energy inefficient. There are two passive methods for antifogging, such as superhydrophilic antifogging [9–15] and hygroscopic antifogging [16–21]. Water droplets on the surface of superhydrophilic materials tend to spread to form water films, thereby achieving the effective antifogging effect. Researchers had successfully utilized the superhydrophilic properties of anatase titanium dioxide, raspberry and mulberry silicas to achieve exceptional antifogging performance [10–14]. However, achieving the superhydrophilic properties of most anatase titanium dioxide required high-temperature calcination and ultraviolet irradiation or the mechanical properties of anatase films prepared at low temperatures were insufficient, while raspberry and mulberry silicas required specific micro- and nanostructures on their surfaces. Additionally, the surface of superhydrophilic coatings is easily contaminated by dust or molecules of low surface energy in the air, leading to a relatively short duration of antifogging. The hygroscopic antifogging coatings, owing to the presence of numerous polar functional groups (such as hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, etc.) on their surface, exhibit the capability to establish hydrogen bonding interactions with water molecules for effectively inhibiting fog formation. However, their antifogging durability, stability and robustness to water, hardness and wear resistance need to be improved. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop long-lasting antifogging coatings.

Using clean and renewable solar energy, photothermal technology has garnered significant attention from researchers. Photothermal materials possess the capability to efficiently convert solar energy into thermal energy [22–26]. Photothermal membranes or coatings had been developed for such applications as catalysis [27], desalination [28], deicing [29–31], and antifogging [32–35]. For instance, Walker et al. [32] used Au nanoparticles and titanium dioxide to prepare coatings with antifogging properties. The temperature of the coating surface rose under sunlight illumination due to the effect of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR). The surface temperature of the coating rose by 3°C under 1 sun, so that the coating exhibited antifogging performance compared to blank sample. 1 sun means the light intensity of 100 mW/cm2. Haechler et al. [33] prepared a coating with a total thickness of about 10 nm by sandwiching an Au nanofilm between two dielectric titanium dioxide nanolayers. The surface temperature of the coating rose by 8.3℃ under 1 sun, showing good antifogging performance outdoors. Li et al. [34] prepared a transparent photothermal coating by embedding cesium-doped tungsten trioxide (CWO) and benzotriazole (BTA) nanoparticles (NPs) in a resin film. The surface temperature of the coating rose by 38°C under 1 sun, which resulted in remarkable antifogging and defogging abilities under extremely frigid or humid conditions. Although there had been successes in sun-driven antifogging, unfortunately, fog was inevitable before an appropriate temperature was reached even under light illumination due to delayed thermal response of photothermal surfaces. To solve these problems of both the short antifogging durability of hygroscopic coatings and the heating lag of photothermal surfaces as result of delayed thermal response, our team designed a double-layer structure of hygroscopic/ photothermal composite coatings and achieved excellent antifogging performance [35]. The hygroscopic material ensured rapid absorption of condensed water droplets on the surface of the coating and inhibited early fog formation. The photothermal material achieved long-lasting antifogging performance under sunlight activation. Although the duration of antifogging had been significantly improved under sunlight illumination [35], the two-step method is generally complex, time-consuming, and may also suffer from weak interlayer bonding. Therefore, it is particularly interesting to prepare efficient photothermal coatings with excellent antifogging performance and mechanical properties through a one-step method at room temperature.

In the current work, polydopamine (PDA)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) coatings with outstanding antifogging performance assisted by sunlight illumination were fabricated by a one-step scrape coating method at room temperature. PDA [36] with excellent photothermal properties and strong adhesion to substrate was applied in combination with PVA as matrix. Carbon dots (CDs) [21], which had previously been found as nano-crosslinkers to form abundant hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups of PVA and substrate, was used to improve the adhesion to substrate, producing a synergistic effect with the PDA. The coatings had an average transmittance exceeding 80 % in the visible region. Under xenon lamp with a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, the temperature of the coating surface rose to 53.1℃, and the antifogging duration was three times more than that without light illumination. The coatings exhibited a pencil hardness of up to 8 H and adhesion-to-substrate of up to 5 A, and it was able to withstand the Taber wear test for 4000 cycles. The preparation method is simple and efficient, may be applicable to a wide range of optical, optoelectronic, medical scenarios.

Anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %) was purchased from Beijing Lanyi Chemical Product Co. Aqueous ammonia (28 wt%) was purchased from Macklin. Poly(vinyl alcohol)(PVA, Mn = 70000–90000 g/mol, 99 % hydrolyzed) was purchased from Aladdin. Aqueous glutaraldehyde (GA, 50 wt% in H2O) was obtained from Shanghai ACMEC Biochemical Co. Dopamine hydrochloride (98 %) was purchased from Innochem. Glass substrates (size: 7.5 cm × 2.5 cm × 0.1 cm) were purchased from Sail Brand. Deionized (DI) water was obtained from a Zhongxin Jiayi ZX-100 purification system (Beijing Zhongxin Jiayi Technology Co.). All chemicals were used without further purification.

4.5 g PVA and 45.5 g DI water were mixed in a three-mouth flask and stirred at 90°C for 30 min. 0.5 g GA (50 wt% in H2O) was mixed with 4.5 g DI water, followed by vigorous stirring at room temperature for 10 min, obtaining a 5 wt% GA solution. CDs was synthesized according to our previous work [21]. Dopamine hydrochloride (1.2 g) was dissolved in 60 mL of DI water. After stirring for 10 min, 0.6 mL ammonia was added, and the solution was stirred at room temperature for 20 h, giving a PDA water dispersion. Finally, the PDA water dispersion was passed through a 0.22 μm filtering membrane before use. Glass substrates were cleaned with DI water and anhydrous ethanol for 3 min, respectively. After drying at 60°C in oven, the glass substrates were treated for 5 min with oxygen plasma (84 W) under an oxygen flow rate of 800 mL min−1. Given volumes of the PVA solution, GA solution, CDs dispersion, and PDA water dispersion were added to a 50 mL beaker. We selected the optimal ratio of PVA, CDs and GA according to our previous work [21]. In the current work, we introduced varied volumes of PDA into the optimized system to investigate the resultant changes in coating properties. The concentrations of these components are summarized in Table S1. The mixture was thoroughly stirred at room temperature for 5 min, followed by ultrasonic deaeration for 2 min. 1.3 mL of the mixture was added and scrape-coated on the plasma treated glass substrates using an FA-202D film coating machine (provided by FUAN ENTERPRISE). After drying at room temperature for 12 h, the coatings were named as M-1, M-2, M-3, and M-4, respectively, and used for characterization and measurements.

Specimens were coated with a layer of gold via ion sputtering before observation on a Hitachi S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope for scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. The morphology and roughness of coating surfaces were examined using a Bruker MM8-SYS scanning probe microscope (AFM). For transmission electron microscope (TEM) observations, products were dispersed in ethanol through sonication for 10 min, and subsequently added onto carbon-coated copper grids. Following drying overnight at 40°C, they were examined using a H-7700 transmission electron microscope with an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. An ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (ThermoFisher Scientific) was utilized for the characterization of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Absorption spectra in the wavelength range of 280 −600 nm was recorded using a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co.). Transmission and reflection spectra in the wavelength range of 300 −2500 nm was recorded utilizing a Carry 7000 UV–VIS–NIR spectrophotometer. The temperature of coatings was measured by a JK808 multi-channel thermocouple temperature tester (Changzhou Jinailian Electronic Technology Co.) and recorded with a handheld Fluke Ti400 infrared thermal imaging camera. Using a Kino SL200B/K automatic contact angle meter, water contact angles (WCAs) were measured by adding 5 μL water droplets onto three different surface areas of samples. FTIR spectra were acquired using a Varian Excalibur 3100 spectrometer, while pencil hardness was evaluated with an Elcometer 3086 Motorized Pencil Hardness Tester. Wear resistance was assessed by a Taber 5135 instrument, and adhesion to the substrate was characterized by tape peeling tests according to the ASTM-D3359 standard test method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and preparation of PDA-PVA coatings
ce:section.ce:para: PDA is a conjugated polymer forming through self-polymerization of dopamine hydrochloride under alkaline conditions. It belongs to a unique class of macromolecules characterized by its extensive π-π conjugated frameworks and highly electron-delocalized structures. These distinctive features endow it with exceptional light absorption capabilities and enable efficient conversion of light into heat [36,37]. Due to its mussel-like characteristics, PDA exhibits strong adhesion to substrates [36]. Therefore, we selected PDA as photothermal material, and PVA as film-forming material to prepare coatings. CDs were applied to enhance the adhesion to substrate in order to produce a synergistic effect with PDA [21]. Well-dispersed CDs were distinctly observed in TEM image (Figure S1a), indicating an average particle size of ca. 10 nm. A one-step scrape coating method at room temperature was used to fabricate PDA-PVA coatings with excellent antifogging performance assisted by sunlight illumination. The preparation process of coatings is illustrated in Fig. 1.

The surface morphology and roughness of coatings were characterized by SEM and AFM techniques. The photothermal hygroscopic composite coatings (M-2, M-3, and M-4) were analyzed. From the top view of SEM ( Fig. 2 a-c), the surface of M-2, M-3 and M-4 coatings are relatively dense, and the distribution of PDA nanoparticles is uniform. The insets reveal that the thicknesses of these three coatings are 39 μm, 40 μm, and 40 μm, respectively. The SEM images of M-1 are presented in Figures S1b and c. Within the same magnification range as M-2, M-3, and M-4, it was observed that the surface morphology of M-1 exhibited relative smoothness (Figure S1b). When we enlarged the image, we observed a large number of nanoparticles on the surface of M-1 (Figure S1c), with particle sizes approximately 10 nm. This observation is consistent with the particle sizes distribution of CDs as measured by our TEM analysis (Figure S1a). It is also evident from Figure S1c that the CDs exhibit a uniform distribution on M-1. As observed from SEM analysis, the particle sizes of CDs are relatively small, whereas those of PDA are comparatively larger.

The chemical components of M-3 were characterized through EDS, and as shown in Fig. 2 d-f, the M-3 primarily consists of carbon (C), oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N) elements. It is evident that the N content is relatively low. AFM images (Fig. 2 g-i) again indicate that the three coatings have a high degree of smoothness. In more detail, the height difference between the highest and lowest points of the three coatings are 11.1, 11.3 and 14.6 nm, and the root-mean-square roughness values are 1.59, 1.62 and 1.97 nm, respectively. Such a smooth feature endows the coatings with exceptional mechanical strength, as will be demonstrated in subsequent tests of their mechanical properties. Furthermore, XPS analysis was utilized to characterize the chemical composition of M-3, as illustrated in Figure S2. The coating mainly consisted of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) with respective proportions of 78.25 %, 20.32 %, and 1.43 %. The binding energies of 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 287.8 eV correspond to the C-C, C-O, and CO bonds, while those of 531.5 eV and 532.8 eV belong the C-O bonds and CO bonds, respectively. The thermal stability of M-3 was investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG). As shown in Figure S3a, the TGA and DTG curves of M-3 could be mainly divided into three parts. The first part (100℃) is probably attributed to the evaporation of water absorbed by the hydrophilic coating. The second part (220 −400℃) might be mostly due to the breaking down of cross-links. And the third part (440℃) could mainly result from the further degradation of the main chains of the polymers [20].

The transparency of any coating must be carefully considered if it would be applied on transparent substrates. The optical transmittance of the four coatings were measured using UV–VIS–NIR spectroscopy in the wavelength range of 300 −2500 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a. After the addition of PDA, significant absorption occurred in the both ultraviolet and visible regions. The average transmittance of the three photothermal coatings (M-2, M-3, M-4) exceeded 80 % in the visible region, achieving an optimal balance between high transmittance in the visible region and absorption in the ultraviolet and visible regions. The maximum and average transmittances for these coatings across both the visible and near-infrared regions are summarized in Table S2.

The presence of short-wavelength, high-energy ultraviolet rays in sunlight can cause sunburn, erythema, itching, and other adverse effects. Prolonged exposure to areas with high levels of ultraviolet radiation poses significant risks to human health. By effectively inhibiting the formation of free radicals and robustly quenching a wide range of free radicals, including •OH, O2•-, •NO and ONOO-, PDA exhibits strong scavenging capabilities against various types of free radicals, thereby possessing remarkable UV shielding properties [37]. Therefore, the addition of PDA into coatings is crucial. As depicted in Fig. 3 b, addition of PDA enabled the M-2, M-3 and M-4 coatings to exhibit pronounced absorption within the ultraviolet spectra in comparison to the M-1 coating without addition of PDA. The absorption of the coatings in both UV and visible regions enhances with increase of PDA concentration. The outstanding UV shielding properties of the coatings may not only enhance the durability of outdoor applications but also provide substantial protection for eyes and equipment.

The wettability of coatings could be quantified by their WCAs. We measured the WCA of coatings, and the results are illustrated in Figure S3b. With increase of PDA concentration, the WCA of the coating gradually decreased, meaning that its hydrophilicity gradually increased. The hydrophilicity (WCA < 90°) of the coatings should have arisen from the abundant hydroxyl groups on PVA and hydroxyl and amino groups on PDA. In fact, as shown in Figure S4a, the peak observed around 3300 −3200 cm−1 in the ATR-FTIR spectra of coatings can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl and amino groups. Additionally, the characteristic peak detected at 1083 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C-O-C after crosslinking. It is worth noting that no characteristic peaks associated with aldehyde groups were observed in these spectra [20,38–40]. Furthermore, peaks at 1410 cm−1 and 1320 cm−1 correspond to the skeleton stretching vibrations of aromatic structures. With increase of PDA concentration, enhancements were observed in the stretching vibration peaks of hydroxyl groups and amino groups (3300 −3200 cm−1), as well as those related to benzene ring skeleton (1410 cm−1 and 1320 cm−1) and C-O-C (1083 cm−1) stretching vibration after crosslinking.

To further check the photothermal properties of the coatings, we monitored change in the surface temperature of blank glass and four different coatings under xenon lamp illumination. The selection of a xenon lamp as light source for simulating controlled sunlight is based on their ability to demonstrate a broadband energy distribution similar to natural light. Fig. 3 c shows a schematic diagram of coating surface temperature and antifogging measurements setup under xenon lamp illumination. Fig. 3 d-f record the surface temperature changes and infrared thermal images of blank glass and four coatings under light intensities of 60, 80, and 100 mW cm−2, respectively (unless otherwise specified, the light intensity refers to that of the coating location). Upon exposure to xenon lamp illumination, all three photothermal hygroscopic composite coatings (M-2, M-3 and M-4) demonstrated a rapid increase in temperature, reaching a plateau within 10 min. The equilibrium surface temperatures of the blank glass and coatings after 10 min of illumination are summarized in Table S3. Infrared thermal images displayed the steady-state temperature of different coatings under xenon lamp illumination at different light intensities for 10 min. Under a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2, the equilibrium surface temperature of M-2, M-3 and M-4 rose to 48.3℃, 53.1℃ and 57.1℃ (Fig. 3 f), meaning an increase of 19.8℃, 24.6℃, and 28.6℃, respectively, as compared to the control sample of blank glass at 28.5℃. The temperature changes of the three photothermal coatings were also recorded under a light intensity of 50 mW cm−2, and the results are shown in Figure S4b. We observed a linear correlation between equilibrium temperature (T) and light intensity (L) in the range of 50–100 mW cm−2 under xenon lamp illumination (Fig. 3 g-i), where the correlation coefficients are 0.9838, 0.9882 and 0.9904, ensuring controlled and safe operation of the coatings in real-world environments [41]. The fitting slopes for M-2, M-3 and M-4 coatings are 0.2439, 0.2949, and 0.3325, respectively. They exhibit an upward trend with the rise in the PDA concentration, implying a corresponding increase in the heating rate. The PDA material demonstrates a high capacity of photon absorption across a wide range of solar wavelengths and efficiently converts light into heat. Very recently, a photothermal coating had been reported to exhibit excellent antifogging performance due to its photothermal effect when the temperature of coating rose by 8.3℃ [33].

We evaluated the antifogging performance of M-1 and M-3 coatings by exposing them to hot steam 5 cm above a 60℃ bath in the absence of xenon lamp illumination. As illustrated in Figure S5, initially, both M-1 and M-3 exhibited excellent antifogging properties due to the presence of abundant polar groups (such as hydroxyl, amino, etc.) in PVA and PDA. However, after 20 min, both M-1 and M-3 became fogging due to exceeding the equilibrium state of water absorption and volume expansion. To evaluate the duration of antifogging performance under a specific light intensity, we tested the antifogging performance using the setup shown in Fig. 3 c, with a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location. As illustrated in Figure S6, after exposing M-1 to this condition for 20 min, fogging was observed. However, within a duration of 30 min, no fogging occurred on the M-3 coating. Clearly, the addition of PDA significantly prolongs the antifogging duration of coatings when exposed to simulated sunlight. We tested the antifogging duration of M-2 and blank glass by exposing them simultaneously to a xenon lamp with a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 and hot steam 5 cm above a 60℃ bath. The blank glass was positioned on the left, while the M-2 was placed on the right, as depicted in Figure S7. A layer of fog quickly obscured the blank glass, obstructing our vision with numerous water droplets. However, the text underneath the M-2 remained clearly visible. The text below M-2 became partially blurred in 50 min of continuous xenon lamp illumination, possibly due to insufficient PDA concentration and thus inadequate photothermal effect for rapid evaporation of water vapor. We also conducted photothermal antifogging performance tests on M-3 and M-4 under identical conditions, as depicted in Fig. 4 and S8. Under these conditions, both M-3 and M-4 showed a durable antifogging capability of more than 60 min. Under a xenon lamp with a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, the antifogging durability was three times more than that without xenon lamp illumination. Because the light transmittance of M-3 was superior to that of M-4, M-3

was utilized for subsequent tests. To validate the extended duration of antifogging performance resulted from photothermal properties, we also conducted tests on the duration of antifogging performance under xenon lamp with a low light intensity of 60 mW cm−2, as shown in Figure S9. The blank glass immediately became foggy when exposed to hot steam, whereas no fog formation was observed on M-3 within 40 min.

The surface temperature of the coating increases when exposed to xenon lamp illumination, which hinders droplet nucleation and effectively prevents fog formation. The results of these experiments validate that the rapid evaporation of droplets was attributed to the efficient photothermal properties exhibited by PDA under xenon lamp illumination. In addition, we tested the light reflectance spectra of the coating before and after water absorption, and the results are shown in Figure S10. When the coating absorbs water, the reflectivity of the coating decreases at 1931 nm, which is the characteristic absorption peak of water. The coating is expected to be utilized in fields such as optical, optoelectronic, medical scenarios, not only for UV ray blocking purposes but also to provide an antifogging effect.

After verifying the outstanding antifogging performance of the photothermal coatings under xenon lamp illumination, we further explored its application in optical devices such as eyeglasses that are prone to fogging. The eyeglass was subjected to plasma pretreatment, utilizing the same method as that employed for the glass substrate (5 min with oxygen plasma at 84 W under an oxygen flow rate of 800 mL min−1). One piece of a pair of eyeglasses was immersed in the mixed hydrosol used for M-3 coating for 5 min and then withdrawn at a specific speed of 20 mm/min, followed by drying at room temperature. Using a film thickness gauge, the thickness of coating was determined on three different locations, and the coating thickness was found to be 4 μm. We placed the coated and uncoated eyeglasses in a −20°C refrigerator for 24 h, then they were exposed to ambient conditions (25°C, 20 −40 %RH) for 5 s before being photographed. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the uncoated eyeglass was immediately obscured by fog. Nevertheless, the words beneath the photothermal coated eyeglass still maintained clear vision. To test the antifogging performance in a real environment, we put the eyeglasses outdoors (Beijing, winter, 2℃) for an antifogging test. We exposed them to hot steam 5 cm above an 80°C water bath for 60 s, and the coated eyeglass maintained clear vision even in outdoor environment (Fig. 5 b). On the other hand, the uncoated eyeglass obstructed the view by creating small droplets of water on its surfaces.

After selecting the M-3 with the best antifogging performance and optical transmittance, the photothermal stability of the coating was further validated by conducting a rigorous light on-off test. Fig. 6 a shows the temperature change of M-3 surface with time in twelve light on-off cycles. Each cycle comprised a 10-min exposure to xenon lamp illumination, followed by 5-min off of the xenon lamp. Under a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, the surface temperature of the coating rapidly increased and then reached a steady state. Upon turning off the xenon lamp, the surface temperature swiftly dropped and returned to room temperature. M-3 demonstrated rapid photothermal response and consistently achieved high equilibrium temperatures during each light on-off cycle test, thereby indicating exceptional photothermal stability for the coating (Fig. 6 b). We further investigated the photothermal properties of coatings with varied thicknesses, as illustrated in Figure S11. It was observed that the surface temperature of the coatings increased with increasing coating thickness. When the coating thickness exceeded 40 μm, it was observed that the heating performance did not significantly improve. Additionally, further increase in thickness would result in unnecessary waste of raw materials. Therefore, we chose the coating with a thickness of 40 μm.

To test the feasibility of the coating in practical applications, we prepared two model houses with roofs of uncoated and coated PMMA substrates. The PMMA model houses were subjected to plasma pretreatment, utilizing the same method as that employed for the glass substrate (5 min with oxygen plasma at 84 W under an oxygen flow rate of 800 mL min−1). We exposed the two model houses to sunlight simulated by a xenon lamp, as shown in Fig. 6 c. The thermocouples were inserted to test the temperature changes of the coatings and the interiors of the model houses. After 600 s of xenon lamp illumination, the temperature of the coated roof and interior rose to 40.1°C and 37.5°C, respectively, exhibiting a significant rise of 8.8°C and 5.0°C compared to the uncoated roof (31.3°C) and interior (32.5°C), as shown in Fig. 6 d-f. In other words, under the xenon lamp simulated sunlight, the photothermal coating increased the temperature of both the roof and interior by 8.8°C and 5.0°C, respectively. This achieved a significant increase of 5°C in the internal environment, which was considered adequate for maintaining standardized heating efficiency in cold regions of the United States [42–44]. The obvious heating of the coating can be visually seen in the infrared images (Fig. 6 g-h). Fluctuation in temperature difference between coated and uncoated model houses here may be related to heat exchange with the outside world. The photothermal coatings are expected to be applied to the field of heating windows, thereby reducing energy consumption in winter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and robustness to water and organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para:
The stability and adhesion to substrate of the coating play an important role in practical applications. However, most antifogging coatings fail to withstand water immersion and tend to separate from the substrate, resulting in the formation of wrinkles. To assess the robustness of the coating to water, M-1 and M-3 were immersed in deionized water for 2 h. No signs of wrinkling were found, as depicted in Figure S12a. We also estimated the weight of M-3 before and after soaking in deionized water for 2 h and calculated water uptake according to Eq. (1). As shown in Figure S12b, the water uptake of M-3 was 395 %.
(1)
Water uptake
%
=
W wet
−
W dry
W dry
× 100 %
Where
W
dry
represents the weight of dry film ,
W
wet
is the weight of wet film after soaking in deionized water for 2 h.

The CDs have abundant amino, carboxyl, and other polar groups, and can be used as nano-crosslinkers to form a large number of hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups of PVA and substrate [21]. Additionally, PDA exhibits mussel-like characteristics and demonstrates strong adhesion to the substrate [36], producing a synergistic effect with the CDs in enhancing the adhesion of the coating to the substrate. In addition, solvent treatment is also a key factor in determining the multifaceted adaptability of these coatings. As shown in Fig. 7 , the coating remains unfolded after being soaked in various organic solvents for 2 h. The solvents from left to right include n-hexane, dichloromethane (DCM), tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethyl acetate (EA), toluene, and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF).

The mechanical properties of coatings are crucial for their practical application. In accordance with ASTM D3363–05, the hardness of M-3 was assessed using the pencil test ( Fig. 8 a). Fig. 8 d displays an optical photograph of M-3 tested with an 8 H hardness pencil, and the coating remained intact without any exposure of the substrate, revealing that the pencil hardness of M-3 was equal to or greater than 8 H. According to ASTM D3359, the adhesion to substrate of M-3 was assessed by making scalpel scratches and peeling off with tape. M-3 was scratched with a scalpel and then adhered to the surface using 3 M tape; the tape was quickly peeled off after applying pressure, as depicted in Fig. 8 b. Even after repeating this process for 100 times (Fig. 8 e), there was no separation between M-3 and the substrate, indicating that its adhesion to substrate was up to 5 A. The wear resistance of M-3 was assessed using a Taber wear tester in accordance with ASTM D4060–01, as depicted in Fig. 8 c. When subjected to 4000 cycles of rotation at 60 rpm by a 0.5 kg abrasive wheel, only a few surface scratches were observed on the coating without any detachment from the substrate (Fig. 8 f), indicating that M-3 could withstand the Taber wear test for 4000 cycles [21]. Our previous work [21] demonstrated that the introduction of CDs can significantly improve the adhesion of the coating to the substrate. The hydroxyl groups of PVA can cross-link with GA or form hydrogen bonds with another PVA. The CDs possess abundant amino, carboxyl, and other polar groups, which can act as nano-crosslinking agents by establishing rich hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups of PVA and substrate, thus significantly enhancing the adhesion to the substrate. In the current work, the CDs not only form a substantial number of hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups present on the substrate and PVA, but they also establish numerous hydrogen bond interactions with the abundant polar groups on PDA, thus enhancing the mechanical properties of the coating. Additionally, PDA exhibits mussel-like characteristics and would exhibit strong adherence to the substrate, producing a synergistic effect with CDs. The polymer cross-linking endows unique mechanical properties to the coating, enabling it to withstand actual scraping [45].

To further characterize the photothermal and antifogging properties after sponge wearing, we applied a 1 kg weight on a sponge and conducted reciprocating movements on M-3 to evaluate its optical transmittance and photothermal properties under varied numbers of wearing cycles. With increase in the number of sponge wearing cycles, there was a slight decrease in the transmittance of M-3 in the visible region (Fig. 8 g), but the transmittance remained consistently above 80 %, indicating its ability to withstand actual friction. After the sponge wearing, the surface temperature of the coating yet rapidly rose to 52°C within 10 min under xenon lamp with a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, demonstrating similar photothermal performance as before sponge wearing (Fig. 8 h). Additionally, we evaluated the photothermal antifogging performance of M-3 after a sponge scrubbing test for 4000 cycles (Fig. 8 i). Even after 4000 cycles of sponge scrub, M-3 showed an antifogging capability of more than 30 min under xenon lamp (100 mW cm−2) illumination. These excellent mechanical properties can be attributed to the crosslinking of PVA, GA and CDs and the connection between the coating components and the substrate through chemical reactions or hydrogen bonds, as well as the strong adhesion properties of PDA.

In summary, PDA-PVA coatings with outstanding antifogging performance assisted by light illumination were fabricated by a one-step scrape coating method at room temperature. PDA with excellent photothermal properties and strong adhesion to substrate was applied in combination with PVA as matrix. CDs, which had previously been found as nano-crosslinkers to form abundant hydrogen bonds with hydroxyl groups of PVA and substrate, were used to enhance the mechanical robustness and the adhesion to substrate of coatings, producing a synergistic effect with the PDA. The obtained coatings had an average transmittance exceeding 80 % in the visible region. Under xenon lamp with light intensities of 60, 80 and 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, the temperature of the coating surface rose to 40.2, 46.1 and 53.1℃, respectively. Under xenon lamp with a light intensity of 100 mW cm−2 at the coating location, the antifogging duration when exposed to hot steam 5 cm above a 60 °C water bath was three times more than that without xenon lamp illumination. The coating remained unaffected even after immersion in water and various solvents for 2 h, without showing any signs of folding or damage. The coating exhibited a pencil hardness of up to 8 H and adhesion-to-substrate of up to 5 A, and was able to withstand the Taber wear test for 4000 cycles. The photothermal and antifogging properties of the coating remained unaffected even after undergoing 4000 cycles of sponge wear. The current method is simple and efficient, and may be applicable to a wide range of optical, optoelectronic, medical scenarios.

Xiuxian Gu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Junhui He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019QY(Y)0503, 2017YFA0207102), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (91963104), Natural Science Foundation of Beijing (L233012), and Joint R&D Laboratory for Functional Agriculture Films.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2025.136170.
